title: Effects of Mindfulness Training and Exercise on Cognitive Function in Older Adults: A Randomized Clinical Trial
headline: Effects of Mindfulness Training and Exercise on Cognitive Function in Older Adults: A Randomized Clinical Tria
summary: A randomized controlled trial of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) examined focus & attention. At 6 months, there was no significant effect of mindfulness training or exercise on episodic memory (MBSR vs no MBSR: 0.44 vs 0.48; mean difference, -0.04 points [95% CI, -0.15 to 0.07]; P = .50; exercise vs no exercise: 0.49 vs 0.42; difference, 0.07 [95% CI, -0.04 to 0.17]; P = .23) or executive function (MBSR vs no MBSR: 0.39 vs 0.31; mean difference, 0.08 points [95% CI, -0.02 to 0.19]; P = .12; exercise vs no exercise: 0.39 vs 0.32; difference, 0.07 [95% CI, -0.03 to 0.18]; P = .17) and there were no intervention effects at the secondary end point of 18 months. There was no significant interaction between mindfulness training and exercise (P = .93 for memory and P = .29 for executive function) at 6 months.
keyFindings: The 2 primary outcomes were composites of episodic memory and executive function (standardized to a mean [SD] of 0 [1]; higher composite scores indicate better cognitive performance) from neuropsychological testing; the primary end point was 6 months and the secondary end point was 18 months.

At 6 months, there was no significant effect of mindfulness training or exercise on episodic memory (MBSR vs no MBSR: 0.44 vs 0.48; mean difference, -0.04 points [95% CI, -0.15 to 0.07]; P = .50; exercise vs no exercise: 0.49 vs 0.42; difference, 0.07 [95% CI, -0.04 to 0.17]; P = .23) or executive function (MBSR vs no MBSR: 0.39 vs 0.31; mean difference, 0.08 points [95% CI, -0.02 to 0.19]; P = .12; exercise vs no exercise: 0.39 vs 0.32; difference, 0.07 [95% CI, -0.03 to 0.18]; P = .17) and there were no intervention effects at the secondary end point of 18 months.
There was no significant interaction between mindfulness training and exercise (P = .93 for memory and P = .29 for executive function) at 6 months.
limitations: Key outcomes were self-reported, which can inflate apparent effects.
Single-trial result; requires replication, and self-reported outcomes may inflate apparent effects.

The practice studied (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared directed-attention component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Effects of Mindfulness Training and Exercise on Cognitive Function in Older Adults: A Randomized Clinical Tria
aiSnippet: RCT, JAMA (2022). The 2 primary outcomes were composites of episodic memory and executive function (standardized to a mean [SD] of 0 [1]; higher composite scores indicate better cognitive performance) from neuropsychological testing; the primary end point was 6 months and the secondary end point was 18 months. At 6 months, there was no significant effect of mindfulness training or exercise on episodic memory (MBSR vs no MBSR: 0.44 vs 0.48; mean difference, -0.04 points [95% CI, -0.15 to 0.07]; P = .50; exercise vs no exercise: 0.49 vs 0.42; difference, 0.07 [95% CI, -0.04 to 0.17]; P = .23)…
whyItMatters: MoM shares directed attention (the focused-attention component) in a brief, guided format with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
